bids.reasoning: At the start of the negotiation, I will aim for a bid with maximum utility according to my preferences since there's no time pressure yet. My most preferred values across all issues are "valueC" due to their highest utilities. I will propose this to see if the opponent is willing to match my ideal bid or counter with their own preferences.

At this early stage in the negotiation process, I aim to maintain offers that are close to my maximum utility. My initial offer gained no traction, and the opponent's offer presents the least beneficial values from my perspective, suggesting a potential high utility demand on their end. Given the low time pressure, I will make minor concessions to encourage reciprocity without drastically reducing my overall utility.

The opponent has been consistently offering low utility to me by choosing the least preferred values across all issues. Despite the time being very early in the negotiation process (t=0.04), I still need to make minor concessions to keep the dialogue open without sacrificing too much utility. I will slightly adjust my previous offer by choosing a mid-utility option for a less important issue, Issue B, to see if this changes the opponent's stance while maintaining the highest utility for more important issues.

At this early stage (t=0.06), I aim to maintain a high utility closer to my ideal preferences while making slight concessions to test the opponent's flexibility. The opponent has consistently offered their least valuable options to me, showing no signs of concession so far. To potentially elicit more meaningful offers from them, I will make a small concession on issueD, where I have shown some flexibility in previous offers, while maintaining high-utility choices on more critical issues like issueF.

At this early stage in the negotiation (t=0.08), the opponent has not yet shown any willingness to concede, as all their offers have remained the same and at their lowest utility for me. Given this, I will maintain a relatively strong position by offering high utility values for my most important issues. However, to show goodwill, I'll make a slight concession on the less important issueB, where I previously offered its second-best value, valueB, and now maintain it. This approach balances maintaining a high utility offer for myself while indicating openness to negotiation.

At this early stage in the negotiation (t=0.1), the opponent has consistently offered their lowest utility values to me, showing no signs of concession. Since my previous highest utility bids have not been reciprocated, I will experiment with a slight trade-off on issueC by offering valueB instead of valueC, which is less critical for me compared to others, in hopes of invoking reciprocation.

The opponent has consistently offered the least favorable values to me, suggesting they may be holding firm or testing my willingness to concede. Given that only a small fraction of time has elapsed (t=0.12) and there's no indication of reciprocation from the opponent, I will keep a strong stance. I propose values that maximize my utility while introducing minimal concessions. I am prioritizing high-importance issues like issueF and issueE while slightly relaxing on issues like issueC and issueD by choosing their second-best options to entice the opponent towards negotiation.

At this stage, the opponent has consistently offered their least preferred values from my perspective, showing no inclination to concede. I'll maintain high-utility values where possible but make concessions on issues with lower weight, like issueC, by selecting the next best value.
